name: Gap vs positional distance
signal: A fixed number of persons sit between two entities.
rule: If n persons sit between A and B, their positional distance is n + 1.
trap: Using the number of people between them as the positional distance.
example: Three persons sit between A and B → distance = 4 positions.

name: Reference reversal
signal: The question asks the reverse of the stated relationship.
rule: If A is k places left of B, then B is k places right of A.
trap: Keeping the same direction after reversing the reference.
example: A is third left of B → B is third right of A.

name: Linked relative chain
signal: Two or more clues share a common entity.
rule: Merge linked relationships into one relative chain before placing it.
trap: Treating each clue independently and losing the shared reference.
example: A left of B; B second left of C → combine A-B-_-C.

name: Fixed anchor
signal: A clue fixes an entity to an end or an exact position from an end.
rule: Use strong anchors when they reduce uncertainty and connect to other clues.
trap: Starting from an isolated clue merely because it appears first.
example: M is fifth from the left end and several clues reference M.

name: Negative constraint
signal: Not adjacent, does not sit, cannot be, or another exclusion.
rule: Use negative information to eliminate candidates, especially after positive placements narrow the board.
trap: Trying to force a direct placement from a clue that only eliminates possibilities.
example: C is not adjacent to D removes the two neighboring positions of D.

name: Controlled case split
signal: A clue creates two genuine possibilities that cannot yet be resolved.
rule: Split only when necessary, keep cases explicit, and prune immediately when a contradiction appears.
trap: Creating cases too early and multiplying unnecessary branches.
example: A sits at one of the extreme ends may require two cases only if no linked clue resolves the end.

name: Completion by elimination
signal: One entity and one legal position remain after meaningful clues are applied.
rule: Infer the final placement. Do not require a redundant clue stating it.
trap: Expecting every final placement to have its own direct clue.
example: If only E and one seat remain, E occupies that seat automatically.
